data.title: Fossil Group: Fighting A Declining Market
data.description:
Fossil Group, Inc. (FOSL) is at an interesting point in time. A secular decline in the fashion watch market and Fossil’s operational missteps have made the company’s earnings performance weak, leading to the stock's sharp long-term decline. The stock has more recently caught the market’s attention, though – Fossil’s shares have doubled during the past year as the company's turnaround plan has shown progress. Insider purchases have reaffirmed the market's faith in a turnaround. Regardless, I remain skeptical of Fossil’s ability to successfully and sustainably turn around earnings.

A Very Brief Introduction of Fossil

Fossil designs, sources, markets, and distributes a range of fashion accessories, primarily watches, through a number of brands. The company owns the Fossil watch brand among other names, but also sells a number of licensed fashion brands, including Michael Kors, Empirio Armani, and others. The brands are primarily priced in an affordable price range.

Setting the Scene: Fossil’s Sales Are on a Steep Decline

The long-term financial performance of Fossil hasn’t been pretty. Sales have been on a near-constant decline from 2015 forward. Fossil’s sales currently stand at $1066 million, down at a sharp -10.7% CAGR from $3229 million in 2015. Fossil guides a mid-teens revenue decline for 2025, and that adjusted operating income will be “breakeven to slightly positive”. Weakening sales continue to cause significant margin pressure, as Fossil is struggling to maintain the cost base at a corresponding level to generate healthy earnings.

Fossil’s concerning trend coincides with the launch of the Apple Watch in 2015 – the market for mechanical fashion watches has been dwindling since. Consumers are shifting from fashion watches to smartwatches for their better functionality. Fossil previously tried to enter the smartwatch category in 2016, but wasn’t ultimately able to compete with Apple (AAPL), Samsung (SSNLF), and others. The rise of smartwatches continues to cause pressure on sales. The rise of dropshipped and white-label watch microbrands has caused increasing competitive pressure in an already weak market.

Fossil hasn’t been the only struggling watch company. Movado Group (MOV) has also had struggles with maintaining earnings. Switzerland’s watch industry federation data has shown a continued decline in the value of watch exports, shown especially sharply in the <CHF 200 (<$255) price range. In November 2025, the lowest price range’s exports declined by -19.9%, continuing a longer trend. The price range’s exports declined by -5.9% in 2024. I previously discussed the trend in an article on Switzerland’s largest watchmaker by unit volume, Swatch Group (SWGAY).

Yet, Fossil’s decline has clearly been the sharpest, questioning Fossil’s brands’ value and the company’s operational strategy.

Making the situation more concerning, Fossil has a significant amount of debt. The company has $176 million in interest-bearing debts, pressuring earnings through interest. Luckily, Fossil managed to refinance its senior notes to 2029 maturity through restructuring. Fossil maintains good liquidity through a $150 million revolving credit facility; debt likely won’t cause immediate liquidity issues, but makes Fossil’s earnings outlook even more volatile to shareholders.

A Turnaround Plan Is in Motion, But the Outlook Is Volatile

The concerning performance led Fossil to unveil a significant multi-year turnaround plan in early 2025. The current plan includes three core pillars – refocusing the core, right-sizing the cost structure, and strengthening the balance sheet. In essence, Fossil is now refocusing the business towards its most profitable areas. Fossil previously branched into too many markets and brands, which the current market environment doesn’t support.

A renewed Fossil brand is one key turnaround driver – a new brand website, new products, heritage brand storytelling, and new marketing campaigns aim to revitalize demand. The Q3 earnings call included some positive remarks on the Fossil brand’s performance from Nick Jonas’s campaign and higher price point products. Semrush’s web traffic data shows stabilization and even some growth for Fossil from mid-2025 forward, but it remains to be seen whether the shift marks the start of a longer term trend.

Renegotiated minimum license royalties and core market prioritization are other key operational changes. Fossil is moving towards a third-party distribution model in its smaller markets, aiming to cut operational costs. Fossil has aggressively cut down on its global direct-to-consumer store network and aims to continue store closures to right-size the business; Fossil has closed 50 stores during the past year, having a total of 204 remaining stores at the end of Q3.

Lower promotions and a focus on higher-margin wholesale partners aim to improve margins. Right-sizing is ultimately expected to cut revenue further to just $800 million by 2027, but to improve Fossil’s margins and to position the company for better long-term growth. Fossil aims to achieve a mid-to-high single-digit adjusted operating margin by 2027.

The changes have clearly been positive so far. While Fossil continues to lose revenues as a result of the turnaround, profitability has already improved clearly. The Q3 report showed a -$14.9 million adjusted operating loss, improving by $7.1 million year-on-year due to significant SG&A cuts. Similar margin progress should continue over the short- to mid-term as Fossil’s channel refocus and other initiatives continue. Fossil’s brand stabilization could bode well if brand indicators continue to show progress.

I believe that the turnaround is still on volatile grounds – Fossil is operating in a very difficult long-term market environment. At some point, Fossil needs to stabilize its revenue base, and visibility for such stabilization is low while Fossil continues to right-size the business. A decade-long decline weighs odds against such stabilization; Fossil, Michael Kors, and Empirio Armani still need to gain a much better brand foothold. Progress on improving profitability is clearly positive, but long-term expectations shouldn’t be set too high.

FOSL Stock Is a Balanced Gamble

I built a discounted cash flow (DCF) model for Fossil to estimate a fair value for the stock. I assume revenues to decline to $800 million for 2027, and to continue on a weak path until 2030 where revenues stabilize at $700 million. The assumption factors in Fossil’s turnaround right-sizing, but also a slow overall brand outlook.

I expect the adjusted EBIT margin to stabilize at 5% from 2027 forward, within Fossil’s target range. The cash flow conversion should stand good after taxes as Fossil has minimal CapEx. Restructuring weighs somewhat on the cash flow outlook, which Fossil will likely require in upcoming years to address declining sales.

The estimates put Fossil’s fair value at $3.8, quite near the stock price as of writing. I believe that the stock’s current valuation makes the investment a balanced risk-to-reward gamble – better-than-expected earnings stabilization could create further upside, but a failed turnaround could deteriorate shareholder value. High debt makes the investment volatile.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 9.04%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I assume an 8.5% long-term interest rate, in line with Fossil’s refinanced notes’ interest. I assume quite a high 50% long-term debt-to-equity ratio.

For cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.23% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.18% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent January estimate for the US. Seeking Alpha estimates Fossil’s beta at 1.35. With a liquidity premium of 0.5%, the cost of equity stands at 10.37% and the WACC at 9.04%.

Takeaway

Fossil’s weak long-term performance is being addressed as the company is on a multi-year turnaround path. Right-sizing the business towards highest-margin businesses has made good progress in stabilizing profitability, but concerning long-term market trends, uncertain brand positioning, thin margins, and high debt make the outlook volatile regardless. As FOSL stock’s valuation seems volatile but balanced at the current level, I initiate the stock at a Hold rating.

data.title: Meta Just Proved The AI Bulls Right
data.description:
Introduction

On January 8, I rated Meta Platforms, Inc. (META) a Strong Buy based on the following thesis:

For me, META is a Buy thanks to its

1. Growth levers of user expansion, broadening monetization & ad placement

2. AI increasing targeting of ads, resulting in pricing and engagement increases

3. Low valuation offers a solid margin of safety.

The stock has done well since, but it is too early to perform a meaningful performance assessment. More importantly, Meta reported their Q4 and thereby FY2025 earnings today in after-market trading, giving us an opportunity to test and potentially change or expand this thesis. The stock is up 8.5% in response, which is already a good sign.

Financials

Meta reported Q4 revenue of $59.9B, up roughly 24% YoY, driven by continued strength in advertising demand across its Family of Apps. Revenue came in clearly ahead of consensus expectations of about $58.3–58.4B, representing a beat of a little over $1.5B. Operating income was $24.7B, implying an operating margin of 41%.

Diluted EPS was $8.88, increasing around 11% YoY. This also exceeded analyst estimates, which were in the low $8.20 range. In my view, these results highlight Meta’s ability to sustain strong top-line growth and profit expansion at scale, even as the company ramps up AI and infrastructure investment.

Organic growth helps lead the way, as Meta’s Family of Apps (Facebook, Instagram, Messenger, WhatsApp) continues to scale, with family daily active people around 3.58 billion, up mid-single digits year-over-year. Ad impressions and average price per ad both increased, supporting revenue growth. A big part of this came from Reels, as mentioned in the earnings call:

Reels had another strong quarter with Watchtime up more than 30% Y/Y in the U.S. Engagement is benefiting from several optimizations we made to improve the quality of recommendations, including simplifying our ranking architecture to enable more efficient model scaling. - CFO Susan Li.

Operating and free cash flows grew 29% and 6.5% YoY, respectively, showing continued strong cash generation. This helps them to continue to invest without having to issue too much debt and buy back some shares at the same time. I like this financial structure much more in the AI trade than some others that solely rely on debt and dilution to fund their future revenue sources, which is much more risky.

Regarding 2026, the company issued the following statement (emphasis added):

We expect first quarter 2026 total revenue to be in the range of $53.5-56.5 billion. Our guidance assumes foreign currency is an approximately 4% tailwind to year-over-year total revenue growth, based on current exchange rates.

We expect full year 2026 total expenses to be in the range of $162-169 billion.

  • The majority of expense growth will be driven by infrastructure costs, which includes third-party cloud spend, higher depreciation, and higher infrastructure operating expenses.
  • The second-largest contributor to total expense growth is employee compensation, driven by investments in technical talent. This includes 2026 hires to support our priority areas, particularly AI, as well as a full year of expenses from 2025 hires.
  • At a segment level, we expect expense growth to be driven by the Family of Apps, with Reality Labs operating losses remaining similar to 2025 levels.

We anticipate 2026 capital expenditures, including principal payments on finance leases, to be in the range of $115-135 billion, with year-over-year growth driven by increased investment to support our Meta Superintelligence Labs efforts and core business.

Despite the meaningful step up in infrastructure investment, in 2026 we expect to deliver operating income that is above 2025 operating income.

Absent any changes to our tax landscape, we expect our full year 2026 tax rate to be 13-16%.

They did not provide any concrete profitability metrics, which makes it harder to forecast future earnings. Operating profit is said to be higher than in 2025, but we do not know by how much. Still, this is a sign of incredible operating leverage when, despite being in the biggest investment cycle Meta has ever been in, skyrocketing operating expenses, an increase in profits can be recorded. What is then going to happen when expenses normalize and the company focuses more on profitability?

AI beneficiary?

In the most direct sense, Meta could be making revenues and profits from selling AI services, as Zuckerberg hinted:

In 2025, we rebuilt the foundations of our AI program. Over the coming months, we're going to start shipping our new models and products. I expect our first models will be good, but more importantly, we'll show the rapid trajectory that we're on. And then I expect us to steadily push the frontier over the course of the year as we continue to release new models. - CEO Mark Zuckerberg.

At the same time, Reality Labs still weighs on profits ($6B loss in Q4 2025), but there is light at the end of the tunnel, as operating losses are expected to inflect soon:

I expect Reality Labs losses this year to be similar to last year, and this will likely be the peak as we start to gradually reduce our losses going forward while continuing to execute on our vision as we plan. - CEO Mark Zuckerberg.

AI is also going to indirectly benefit Meta, by increasing ad pricing, which I outlined in my previous coverage, but also by significantly boosting employee efficiency. If the whole organization of tens of thousands of employees gets much more done, the business can grow so much faster. With AI still developing and improving, efficiency gains should also become larger from here.

Since the beginning of 2025, we've seen a 30% increase in output per engineer, with the majority of that growth coming from the adoption of AI-generated coding, which saw a big jump in Q4. We're seeing even stronger gains with power users of AI coding tools whose output has increased 80% year over year. We expect this growth to accelerate through the next half. - CFO Susan Li.

Valuation

At a forward P/E ratio of just 23, META's organic growth levers, high margins, capital efficiency, and operating leverage seem almost fully disregarded. There are also no Reality Labs or direct AI product profits priced into the stock, which now seem more likely to manifest sometime in the future. Even if they continue at a loss, the company is undervalued. After another strong report today, it's likely that analysts will revise their EPS targets upwards, making the "real" forward P/E ratio even smaller. The same is true for their P/OCF ratio, a metric to gauge how much cash they are able to produce once CapEx normalizes, is at 13.4, which is very low as well. Both of these imply upside potential just from a multiples perspective.

Risks

As noted above, losses from Reality Labs and AI spending might continue to drag down META's profits longer than expected and specifically at a lower ROI than expected. Zuckerberg is not particularly known for a solid track record of great capital allocation. The worst case of that, however, would likely be a small underperformance, and once these segments are divested or shut down, the stock can perform well again.

Another aspect is that despite the growing number of daily active people, saturation of users could be reached in a few quarters or years, as there are only so many billion people on this earth. What directly ties into that are proposed bans of social media and restrictions globally, specifically for teens. The fact that the CFO addresses this in the earnings report shows it is a significant business risk for Meta.

Finally, we recently aligned with the European Commission on further changes to our Less Personalized Ads offering, which we will begin rolling out this quarter. However, we continue to monitor legal and regulatory headwinds in the EU and the U.S. that could significantly impact our business and financial results. For example, we continue to see scrutiny on youth-related issues and have a number of trials scheduled for this year in the U.S., which may ultimately result in a material loss. - CFO Susan Li.

Conclusion

The Meta long thesis remains largely the same, as they proved again what the business is capable of, though some upside optionality is added from AI. Meta remains a great investment for the long run at these prices, even after the post-earnings spike.

Rating: Maintain Strong Buy.

data.title: Apple Earnings Preview: Investors Eye Record Revenue And AI Updates Amidst Market Jitters
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

Apple Inc. (AAPL) prepares to report its fiscal first quarter 2026 results on Thursday, January 29, after market close. Wall Street will be watching with intrigue, no doubt, as despite a sluggish start to the year for the stock, which is down roughly 6-9% year-to-date, expectations are high.

Analysts project the tech giant will deliver its largest year-over-year revenue jump in four years, driven by a robust holiday season, the iPhone 17 cycle, and double-digit growth in its Services division.

What to Expect?

Wall Street consensus points to a record-breaking quarter for Apple.

  • Revenue: Analysts expect revenue to land around $138.5 billion, representing a growth rate of approximately 10-12% year-over-year. This would easily eclipse the previous record of $124.3 billion set in Q1 2025.
  • Earnings Per Share (EPS): Consensus estimates for EPS are pegged at $2.67, up roughly 11% from $2.40 in the year-ago quarter.

Gross Margin: Investors will look for margins to remain steady between 47-48%, though any impact from rising memory costs or tariffs will be scrutinized.

Key Areas to Focus On - Segment Performance Analysis

While the headline numbers are critical, the narrative during the conference call, specifically regarding future demand and AI, will likely dictate the stock's immediate reaction.

  • iPhone 17 Demand and the Supercycle: The iPhone remains the engine of Apple’s profitability, and Q1 covers the critical holiday sales period. Analysts forecast iPhone revenue to surge by over 12% to approximately $78-$80 billion.

The "supercycle" thesis relies heavily on the iPhone 17 significantly outperforming its predecessors. Early data suggests strong demand for the Pro models, and investors will want confirmation that the upgrade cycle is accelerating, particularly among the estimated 300+ million users with iPhones older than four years.

  • The China Rebound: China has been a battleground for Apple, facing stiff competition from domestic rivals like Huawei and a mixed economic backdrop. However, recent data offers a glimmer of hope: Counterpoint Research indicates Apple may have led the Chinese smartphone market in Q4 with over 20% market share.

A confirmed rebound in Greater China revenue would be a major bullish signal, alleviating fears that Apple is losing its grip on its most important international market.

  • Services Growth: The Services segment (App Store, iCloud, Apple TV+, etc.) is Apple’s profit growth engine. Wall Street is looking for revenue to hit roughly $30 billion, implying ~14% growth.

Sustained double-digit growth here is vital to justify Apple’s premium valuation (approx. 30x forward earnings). Any deceleration could spook investors who view Services as the company's safety net against hardware cyclicality.

  • AI Strategy and "Apple Intelligence": Artificial Intelligence remains the "wild card." While Apple has been quieter than its peers, the integration of "Apple Intelligence" and the partnership with Google (Gemini) to power Siri are central to the long-term thesis.

Market participants will listen intently for updates on consumer adoption of AI features and, more importantly, how Apple plans to monetize them. Commentary on future capital expenditures related to AI servers will also be key.

The path forward is not without significant obstacles. The global memory shortage, the ongoing DOJ antitrust trial, and the complexities of the European regulatory environment present real risks to the company's operating model. The "trillion-dollar tightrope" involves balancing these external pressures while continuing to deliver the "quality and resilience of earnings" that investors have come to expect.

If Apple can demonstrate that it has successfully harnessed the power of generative AI to drive both hardware upgrades and high-margin recurring fees, its premium valuation may be well-justified for years to come.

The more seasoned market participants are watching three main things: whether the sales recovery in China will last, how many people are buying the new AI-powered Pro phones, and the company's plan to make Siri the main controller of a system that is so connected, users will find it very hard to leave.

Potential Implications for the Stock Price

Apple stock is currently trading near a technical support level, testing its October 2025 lows. The market sentiment has been cautiously optimistic, but valuation concerns persist.

  • Bullish Scenario: A "beat and raise" (beating estimates and raising guidance) combined with positive commentary on China and AI could spark a reversal, pushing the stock back toward the $270-$288 range. Analysts at JP Morgan recently raised their target to $315, citing confidence in the multi-year product cycle.
  • Bearish Scenario: If iPhone revenue misses the high expectations or if guidance for the March quarter is tepid due to rising component costs (specifically memory), the stock could break below crucial support levels near $206-$210.

Apple Daily Chart, January 28, 2025

The Bottom Line

This earnings report is about more than just a holiday quarter beat, it is a litmus test for the iPhone 17's longevity and Apple's ability to maintain growth in the AI era. With the stock currently underperforming the broader tech sector in 2026, a strong report is needed to reignite investor momentum.

Original Post

data.title: Meta: Shock And Awe (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

After delivering a triple play for Q4 2025, Meta Platforms, Inc. (META) stock is rising by ~9% to $729 per share in the post-market session. In this report, we break down Meta's latest quarterly results and reassess its long-term risk/reward to reach an informed investment decision.

Dissecting Meta's Q4 2025 Report

For Q4 2025, Meta reported revenues of $59.89B [+24% y/y] and non-GAAP EPS of $8.88 per share, beating consensus estimates of $58.47B and $8.18 per share, respectively.

And, these stronger-than-expected results were driven by continued improvement across Meta's core KPI metrics, with Family of Apps [FOA] DAPs up 7% y/y to 3.58B, FOA Ad Impressions up 18% y/y, and FOA Average Price Per Ad up +6% y/y in Q4 2025.

Now, while Meta exceeded management's CAPEX guide for 2025 of $70-72B by a sliver, total expenses fell within management's guided range of $116-118B. Consequently, Meta delivered operating income of ~$25B, operating cash flow of ~$36B, and free cash flow of ~$14B for Q4 2025.

According to Mark Zuckerberg, Meta founder and CEO,

We had strong business performance in 2025. I'm looking forward to advancing personal superintelligence for people around the world in 2026.

Looking forward, Meta's leadership is guiding for Q1 2026 revenue to come in the range of $53.5-56.5B, well above the pre-ER consensus of $51.4B, thereby completing the triple play for Q4.

Interestingly, Meta's total expense guide of $162-169B calls for a 40% y/y jump in expenses in 2026, with CAPEX expected to rise to $115-135B (from ~$72B in 2025) to support Meta's ongoing quest for superintelligence.

Despite management projecting 2026 operating income to be higher than 2025, I think such heavy CAPEX spending will raise several question marks around ROI in the investor community, given that expense growth is far outpacing revenue growth.

Now, that said, the social media/digital advertising giant has a cash-rich balance sheet and an incredibly cash-generative core business. Furthermore, in order to keep investors on board, Zuckerberg & Co. appear to be taking a step back on Reality Labs (eliminating cash burn growth and ~10% of jobs) and pausing stock buybacks, which fell to nil in Q4 2025. The dividend was held steady in Q4, at $1.34B [$0.525 per share].

Is META Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

In my previous note on the company, I upgraded META stock to a "Buy" after a post-Q3 earnings dip to the mid-to-high $600s:

In our previous update in July, I downgraded Meta to a Hold rating, citing suboptimal long-term risk/reward. However, since then, we have seen a solid time (Meta made decent business progress in Q3) and price correction (Meta stock has declined from $775 per share to $685 per share). Consequently, Meta's 5-year expected CAGR return has improved from ~12% to ~16%, and now exceeds TQI's investment hurdle rate of 15%.

From a valuation standpoint, Meta is once again a Buy.

Now, from a technical perspective, Meta's post-ER selloff is damaging near term; however, the medium-to-long-term setup is still quite bullish, with Meta potentially in the middle of a bullish Wave-5 move as per Elliot Wave Theory, with a target range of $850-1075 per share.

Considering Meta's healthy business performance, reasonable valuation, and constructive technical setup, I am upgrading Meta back to a Buy rating in light of its Q3 2025 earnings dip.

Key Takeaway: I rate META stock a Buy in the high-$600s.

Source: Meta: Buy The Q3 Earnings Dip (Rating Upgrade)

And, with Meta stock jumping up ~9% to upwards of $729 in today's after-hours session, I think my previous buy call was a prescient one.

Now, sticking to our reasonable, long-term growth and margin assumptions, Meta's updated fair value is ~$736.5 per share [or ~$1.89T market cap]. With Meta stock trading right around this level, I believe META is now fairly valued.

Now, predicting where a stock will trade in the short term is impossible; however, over the long run, a stock will track its business fundamentals and obey the immutable laws of money. If the interest rates were to return to artificially low levels (i.e., ZIRP), higher equity multiples would be justifiable. However, I work with the assumption that interest rates will eventually track the long-term average of ~5%. Inverting this number, we get a trading multiple of ~20x (P/FCF).

Assuming a base-case P/FCF (exit) multiple of ~20x, I see Meta stock rising from $729 per share to $1438 per share at a CAGR of ~14.5% over the next five years.

As you may know, TQI's investment hurdle rate is 15%. With Meta failing to exceed this level on the back of its post-Q4 surge, it is no longer a buy under our valuation process. The patient long-term investor who is willing to accept slightly lower returns can still allocate fresh capital here, but as a rules-based investor, I am going to have to wait for a better entry point or a time correction to increase my allocation to META.

With Meta's infrastructure spending set to outpace its revenue expansion rates, I can see Mr. Market affording us another bite at the cherry in the next 6-12 months. Also, from a technical perspective, Meta appears to be in a 5-wave EWT move to the downside right now, which points to a culmination in the ~$425-575 range.

Considering the balance of business fundamentals, valuation, and technicals, I am downgrading META stock back to a "Hold" rating.

Key Takeaway: I rate META stock a Hold in the mid-$700s.

Thanks for reading, and happy investing. Please share your thoughts, concerns, and/or questions in the comments section below.

data.title: Airline Stocks Hit By Record Cancellations: Why This Isn't As Bad As It Looks
data.description:
As major parts of the US have been plagued by winter storms, many airline flights have been cancelled. On Sunday, the peak was reached, marking the highest number of cancellations since the pandemic. In this report, I have a look at the number and assess which airlines were most affected and whether it is a major reason for concern for the airline financials for the first quarter.

A Look At The Airline Winter Storm Cancellation Numbers

A good point to start is by looking at the cancellations and delayed flights by day. This includes cancellations for US and Canadian airlines, which are most affected. In total, nearly 21,000 flights were cancelled between Sunday and Tuesday, and the number of delays was approximately the same. On Friday and Saturday, another 9,000 flights were cancelled.

If we look at the top 10 airlines with the most cancellations by day, we see that American Airlines was consistently among the most affected airlines driven by hub positioning followed by Delta, Southwest, and United.

If we pivot the table and graph again, we see that American Airlines by far had the highest number of cancellations driven by stickiness of the cancellations, followed by Delta, Southwest, and United.

More meaningful is not to look at the highest total number of cancellations, but at the cancellation percentages. What we see is that American Airlines had the highest cancellation rates. The other mainline carriers did substantially better, with cancellation rates between 15 and 25 percent. JetBlue was also heavily affected, with 45% of flights cancelled, and the regional airlines that operate through capacity purchase agreements were also affected.

Comments From American Airlines

With American Airlines being the most affected airline, it is also helpful to take a look at their comments:

For the first quarter, capacity is projected to be up 3% to 5% year-over-year as we maximize the value of our network through stronger schedules in many of our hub cities. This is inclusive of approximately 1.5 points impact from Winter Storm Fern.

So, in terms of capacity hit, we do see a hit that the company really feels in its Q1 performance. The topline hit is expected to be between $150 million and $200 million, with a hit on cost per available seat-mile excluding fuel having the same impact as the capacity deployment hit. That is not odd, as the costs excluding fuel largely remain the same, and the hit is primarily on the top line. JetBlue had to cancel over 1,100 flights in total, but does not expect any major hit from the storm to its full-year targets.

So generally, there is a financial hit. However, it is already quite limited for American Airlines at 1.5% in missed revenues. So, mostly we will see any pressure on the top line but not necessarily material for the other mainline carriers. The regional carriers saw elevated cancellation rates as well, but we note that these operate under capacity purchase agreements, so the exposure is limited. CPAs get paid by block hours flown with minimum block hour guarantees. So, if there are adverse weather events, then there is some revenue hit that is mostly captured through payment of close to the minimum guarantees. There is some potential cost hit from crew pay and aircraft repositioning, but I do not expect a major hit to these carriers despite the elevated cancellation rates.

A Look At Airline Stock Performance

If we look at the performance of the airline ETF, US Global Jets, we see that it has underperformed the global markets. The ETF lost 3.8% while the market gained 3.8%. The two names that were most affected by the winter weather, namely American Airlines and JetBlue, also saw the steepest declines in the share prices. However, we also note that these companies reported during the winter storm, and so the share price reaction is not a pure reflection of the elevated cancellation rates.

Overall, I am not concerned about any major financial hit. We see limited hits for American Airlines, and so for the other airlines, I expect the hits to be substantially smaller. Q1 is always the softest quarter for airlines, so that is not really where an airline makes or breaks a year unless it sees weeks of disruptions. Airlines also assume certain completion factors that are lower for Q1, as some adverse weather is being factored in.

Conclusion: No Major Concern For Airline Stocks

While adverse weather does have a financial impact, I believe there is no reason to be worried about valuation at this point or any major downside to performance. Winter weather, even when it is more severe than expected, is a reality of Q1, and the financial performance is mostly delivered in the subsequent quarters.

data.title: Meta Platforms Q4 Review: AI Train Full Steam Ahead
data.description:
Introduction

Meta Platforms, Inc. (META) just reported great Q4 and full-year results and gave us a look into future capex spending for the year. I wanted to go through the report and give some comments on the outlook.

It’s been over 2 years since the last time I covered Meta, so it is a good time to revisit. Since then, the company's stock has doubled in share price, which is not as impressive as other AI companies have been since that time.

By the Numbers

Sales topped close to $60B, up a very respectable 24% y/y, beating estimates by $1.4B. In terms of what drove such performance, we can see that DAP increased 7% to 3.58B, ad impressions increased by 18% y/y, and average price per ad increased 9% for the year. Advertising, the company’s main revenue generator, continues to perform extremely well, no matter the environment, boosting sales by that 24% y/y.

Reality Labs continues to disappoint and is mostly irrelevant when looking at the revenue segments as a whole. Except if we look at the profitability numbers. Operating margins for the Family of Apps came in at 51.3% of total revenues and over $30.8B in dollar terms, while RL operating loss continues to increase to -$6B from -$5B in 2024. This underperformance continues to drag on META’s profitability, but even then, total operating income margin for the three months and the full year was 41.3% and 41.4%, respectively.

As I have said in the past, META can afford to mess around with RL and try to make something out of it. But I think CEO Mark Zuckerberg would be better off dropping it already. That’s just my opinion. Q4 GAAP EPS came in at $8.88, which beat estimates by a respectable margin of $0.66.

Cash flow from operations this quarter came in at around $36B, up almost 30% compared to last year, and ended the year with $115.8B vs. $91.32B last year. Free cash flow for the quarter was around $14B, which is surprisingly higher than last year’s $13.15B. What’s not surprising is the company’s full-year free cash flow came in at $43.6B vs. $52.1B last year. This is where we see the impact of increased capex spending in the AI race, which continues to have a lot of steam going forward. The capex for the year was $72B, as was expected.

Looking at the company’s financial position, we can see that the balance sheet remains robust, with over $81B in cash, equivalents, and marketable securities against $58B in long-term debt, which for such a mega cap like META is peanuts. I’ve always liked how strong the balance sheet of META is; nothing has changed.

On top of these results, the company issued guidance for the next quarter as well. Sales should be in the range of $53.5B-$56.5B, or $54.9B at the midpoint. This number translates to around 30% y/y growth for Q1. Fantastic growth is still ahead. Analysts were expecting the guidance to be around $51.3B at the midpoint, so Zuckerberg blew past expectations.

Furthermore, we finally got some concrete numbers instead of the past comments like “notably higher,” which didn’t tell us that the capex is going to almost double at the top of the range. The range Zuckerberg gave us is $115B-$135B. That is notably higher than the 2025 capex, alright.

Overall, it seems that the market loved these results, as well as the company’s guidance for the next quarter, and gave us an actual number for capex, which I’m pretty sure everyone was looking forward to the most. Including me. In this report, META’s after-market price action is showing up 9% so far. At the same time, Microsoft (MSFT) has reported its numbers, and even though overall results were great, a beat on the top and bottom line, shares are down around 5% as of right now, and surprisingly, nothing was mentioned for capex going forward. Probably a lot of investors were disappointed about that specific metric.

Comments on the Outlook

Staying on the topic of importance, the capex spending for 2026 is enormous. There is no stopping this AI train for now, and any company that is involved in one shape or another that makes these hyperscalers’ dreams come true will have another stellar year ahead. Over the next couple of weeks, we will hear other big tech companies’ results and will be anticipating to hear specifically about AI capex spend. I am sure none of them are going to bow down and will continue to shovel hundreds of billions of dollars into this madness. Meta’s $72B capex spending was one of the lowest if we compare it to the likes of Amazon’s (AMZN) $125B, MSFT’s $95B, and Alphabet’s (GOOG) around $92B. So, this can only mean that META is probably going to be at the bottom of CapEx spending for this year once again.

META is more or less all-in right now in AI and will continue to chase leadership. Reality Labs’ recent pivot helps with that goal, too. The company recently laid off over 1,000 employees in VR/Metaverse teams, which means now the company is able to redirect over $18B to other ventures like AI-powered AR glasses and other XR wearables with Llama integration, which have seen quite a bit of demand over the last while. The products sell.

Going back to Capex, however, Meta is going to invest $600B by 2028 to support its AI data center infrastructure, so I expect Capex to continue to climb well past 2026.

Zuckerberg continues to approach this AI race very aggressively and will do anything it can to be the leader. He wants to achieve superintelligence, and with such deep pockets, I say by all means, keep going. I am very interested to see what this madman can come up with.

The company’s family of apps continues to be the go-to for many, if not most, advertisers, and, of course, I don’t see why not. The reach is tremendous, and the costs are competitive with good conversion rates. The company is fully embracing AI ad tools that help advertisers convert more people, boosting ROI due to better targeting/optimization techniques. It’s like the whole iOS privacy issue was never an issue, looking at these growth numbers.

So, the Family of Apps will continue to generate massive cash flow to fund Zuckerberg’s AI dreams going forward, and maybe we’ll get a profitable product from its Reality Labs business eventually. I’m not holding my breath, and I think if you are all in on AI, you might as well continue to pivot away from VR products into something that seems to be gaining some traction, like the smart glasses. The segment has lost around $75B in operating income since 2020. I think it’s time to cut the fat and focus on something that might have a chance. The Ray-Ban Meta AI smart glasses have seen unprecedented demand in 2025, helping production ramp to 20m/year.

Additionally, the company is looking to monetize its family of apps further, as it is beginning to test paywalled premium sharing and AI features. It’s still not clear what this monetization is going to look like, but it seems that there will most likely be some AI-exclusive offerings, like the AI agent Manus, Vibes AI video gen, and other app-specific perks, like Instagram anonymous Stories, WhatsApp ad-free status, and Facebook’s advanced analytics.

Honestly, I’m not sure how many will opt in, but given the reach of the family of apps, even if, let’s say, 5% opt in, that translates to around $10B in ARR at around $5 dollars a sub. Meta Verified is around $15, so maybe $5 a month is conservative? Time will tell, but that is billions of dollars in revenue that was not there before, and with minimal costs, I would assume.

Is Meta a Buy Right Now?

I believe that since the AI race continues to run full steam ahead, the company’s share price will see further upside, mostly based on AI hype. The company is going to spend hundreds of billions more on its superintelligence dreams. I can see it reaching $2T-$3T in market cap over the next couple of years, but for me, it is a bit too much for now.

My portfolio is already heavily biased towards AI through other AI-adjacent companies, so for me, it is not a good time to buy. For that reason, I will stay on the sidelines and will think of adding on a decent correction in the future. Also, I don’t see a reason to sell right now, when the company performed quite badly over the year, and with AI euphoria running ever higher, so hold onto your shares and enjoy the ride.

data.title: Fed Watch: Can I Place You On Hold?
data.description:
By Kevin Flanagan

As was widely expected, the Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) decided to pause their rate cuts at the January meeting, keeping the fed funds trading range at 3.50%-3.75%.

For those keeping track, the Fed had lowered 'the funds rate' by 75 basis points (bps) during the final three FOMC meetings of 2025. Now, the question becomes, is this just a pause that 'refreshes,' or are policymakers shifting their decision-making process to the next phase?

Count me in the camp that the Fed is reassessing their stance on rates. I would agree with the notion that monetary policy is still skewed more than likely towards further easing, but I would also recognize that the financial markets will be operating in a scenario where rate cuts are either near or at the end of this easing cycle.

Based upon the broader economy coming out of 2025, there did not appear to be a need to enter an 'accommodative phase' for policy, but perhaps just get back to 'neutral'. This is a point Powell & Co. have been making as well. Now, what is a neutral fed funds rate? That is the key question. If you believe it begins at perhaps 3.50%, then we are essentially at neutral, or very close.

Why are Fed rate cuts potentially in the latter innings? Since this rate cut cycle began in September 2024, Powell & Co. have reduced fed funds by a total of 175bp. Perhaps more importantly in terms of reference, let's go back to my opening paragraph: the Fed has cut rates by 75bps just over the last three to four months.

In other words, you can make the case the Fed may now be far less behind the curve than where they were pre-September. Or, taking it even one step further, perhaps monetary policy is no longer behind the curve.

This point has been underscored by many of the regional Fed bank presidents, and at times, has created an impression of the Fed being a 'house divided.' This development could be on display throughout the 2026 FOMC meeting calendar.

While current Chairman Powell will be at the helm for two more FOMC gatherings this year, a new Fed Chair will be leading the way for the remaining eight meetings this calendar year, assuming, of course, Trump's nominee gets approved on a timely basis. As a result, as we saw at the December FOMC convocation, multiple dissenters could become more of the norm as compared to what investors are accustomed to seeing.

The Bottom Line

Again, I can't emphasize enough how the Fed, and of course the money and bond markets, are now back to being highly data dependent. While the Fed operates under its dual employment and inflation mandate, it continues to appear that the labor market data is more of the 'wild card' for future policy decisions. With inflation still almost a percentage point above the Fed's own 2% target, the FOMC will need to see additional cooling in the labor markets to spur additional rate-cutting action.

Kevin Flanagan, Head of Investment Strategy

As leader of WisdomTree's Investment Strategy group, Kevin serves as Head of Investment Strategy, as well as the Head of Fixed Income Strategy. In this role, he is on the Investment Committee, writes macro & fixed income-related content and works closely with the sales, research and marketing teams. In addition, Kevin conducts client-facing webinars & meetings, providing expertise on WisdomTree's existing and future bond ETFs. Prior to joining WisdomTree, Kevin spent 30 years at Morgan Stanley, where he was Managing Director and Chief Fixed Income Strategist for Wealth Management. He was responsible for tactical and strategic recommendations and created asset allocation models for fixed income securities. He was a contributor to the Morgan Stanley Wealth Management Global Investment Committee, primary author of Morgan Stanley Wealth Management's monthly and weekly fixed income publications, and collaborated with the firm's Research and Consulting Group Divisions to build ETF and fund manager asset allocation models. Kevin has an MBA from Pace University's Lubin Graduate School of Business, and a B.S. in Finance from Fairfield University.

Original Post

data.title: Tesla Stock: Pie In The Sky Valuations And Falling Sales - But Don't Sell It Short
data.description:
A lot has happened since my last article about Tesla, Inc. (TSLA), titled “Tesla’s stock should be 10 times cheaper” and published on 12 August 2025. The stock traded for about $340 per share, while now it is selling for $442 per share.

But what exactly has happened during this time period? In fact, Tesla has reported a poor September 2025 earnings quarter with low net income. Moreover, in the beginning of January 2026, it reported its second consecutive year of falling deliveries. This is crucial for Tesla because a lion’s share of its sales and earnings are still due to electric vehicle deliveries.

Finally, as I am writing this, the EV maker has just reported a disappointing set of Q4 2025 results. That is why TSLA stock should be bought neither by a value investor nor by a growth investor. At the same time, it is a horrible stock to sell short. Let me explain why.

Recap of my previous article

In my previous article about Tesla, I wrote that Tesla's stock was highly overvalued. I also wrote that the excessive valuations were partly due to the hopes that Tesla would successfully launch a full self-driving model. I was quite skeptical of such a possibility. In this publication I also mentioned that neither Tesla's sales nor its EPS was growing.

Despite the full self-driving ambitions expected to come true as early as autumn 2025, Tesla’s full self-driving, or FSD, still requires plenty of improvements. Nothing good is developing on the earnings and sales front either. I will explain this later in this article.

Deliveries

Electric vehicles are still accountable for a lion’s share of the company’s sales. As of 3Q 2025, sales revenues coming from selling EVs totaled approximately $21.2 billion. The company’s total sales revenues over the same reporting period totaled about $28.1 billion. That is why maintaining high EV deliveries is so important for Tesla.

As can be seen from the table and graph below, the company’s EV deliveries were at their highest in 2023 but then started declining.

Year

2020

2021

2022

2023

2024

2025

Deliveries

499550

936172

1313851

1808581

1789226

1635448

Source: Prepared by the author based on Tesla’s data

However, in Q3 2025, the deliveries rose by 7% because most consumers wanted to take advantage of the tax credit advantage before it expired on 30 September 2025. For Q4 2025, the deliveries declined compared to Q3 2025, also because there was no opportunity to receive this benefit.

Q4 2025 results and earnings history

In short, the Q4 2025 results were poor, just like they had been in the past several quarters. The total automotive revenues declined year-on-year by 11%, totaling $17,693 million; the total sales were $24,901 million, a decline of 3% year-on-year. But the net income performed the worst, totaling only $840 million, a decrease of 61%.

Obviously, the situation with sales revenues is as complicated as with the company’s EV deliveries simply because, as I have mentioned above, they are accountable for a large proportion of the company’s revenue streams.

Tesla’s quarterly sales and earnings (in $million)

Also, if we see the graph above, we will see that the company’s quarterly sales have stopped rising after 2022. However, in Q3 2025, these increased. But this effect was primarily due to the tax credit effect that I have explained above. It was highly likely a one-off.

Most unpleasant is the situation with the net income figures. The numbers are lingering near the levels seen in 2021. In other words, there is no growth. But it seems the numbers are declining instead.

The same is true of the annual earnings figures given in the table and the graph below. The net income of 2024 was two times lower than the net income of 2023. The same is true of 2025—the net income is two times lower than it was in 2024.

Tesla’s annual sales and earnings (in $ million)

Year 2020 2021 2022 2023 2024 2025
Sales 31536 53823 81462 96773 97690 94831
Net income 721 5519 12556 14997 7280 3794

Source: Prepared by the author based on Tesla's data

The trend is obvious here.

Other news and downside risks

Tesla is still far away from the full self-driving target announced last year by CEO Elon Musk. He expected the company to launch a model with the FSD feature as early as September 2025. However, on 8 January 2026, Tesla’s CEO wrote that in order to achieve full self-driving, it is necessary to have 10 billion miles of training data, which is a very long way to go, to say the least, because by the end of December 2025, the company has only accumulated 7 billion miles of data.

Moreover, although it seems to be hard to believe, Tesla is facing stiff competition. Even though the company led by Elon Musk has a very strong and widely recognized brand image, it faces competition from Chinese EV makers, most notably BYD (BYDDY). In fact, it lost its top position to the Chinese EV manufacturer in 2025.

Moreover, most traditional automakers like Ford (F) and General Motors (GM) produce electric vehicles in addition to the ones powered by petrol. So, they are also competitors to Tesla.

Competition is even true of the full self-driving feature. For example, Nvidia (NVDA) is planning to enter the self-driving car business with its “Alpamayo” AI platform.

On 30 September 2025, the U.S. government ended the $7,500 tax credit benefit for buying or leasing new EVs. The $4,000 credit for used EVs also ended on that day. This decision was not reflected in Tesla’s 3rd quarter 2025 earnings. In fact, the sales rose in 3Q 2025 because consumers rushed to take advantage of the $7,500 benefit.

Also, as reported by Reuters, in December 2025, Tesla's sales in Germany decreased by almost 50% compared to the same month in 2024. By contrast, Chinese EV manufacturer BYD’s deliveries increased more than 12-fold to 4,109 units in December, totaling 23,306 units for the whole of 2025.

There is also a clear disconnect between these rather poor fundamentals and excessive stock valuations that I will discuss in some more detail later in this article.

Upside factors

Upside factors are, however, also quite straightforward. First and foremost is the company’s strong brand image and its loyal army of fans—both consumers and investors. Despite the obviously poor performance of the EV makers in the last two years, its stock price is nearing all-time highs, which shows TSLA stock’s resistance to the company’s changing fundamentals. That is the main reason why I think that TSLA stock is a horrible stock to sell short.

One more upside risk factor I see is the fact that Tesla operates in the innovative sector. Despite the corporation’s reliance on its EV sales to generate income, Elon Musk is trying to make Tesla shift to producing robotaxis and humanoid robots. Here I personally see a lot of uncertainty but also the company’s growth potential.

As you can see from the diagram above borrowed from Investing.com, in 2019 Tesla’s stock price was about 20 times lower than it is now. The corporation’s sales were poor, the cash reserves were not brilliant, and some analysts even said that Tesla might “fold.” However, since then the company’s sales surged, and its net income improved, which made the corporation’s stock price surge to incredible highs. So, betting against Tesla’s business is also a significant risk, in my opinion. As I have mentioned above, it operates in an innovative sector, and it is unclear how the company might monetize its innovation potential in the future.

Valuations

As I have mentioned many times before, all of Tesla’s valuation ratios are over and above the sector’s medians. The corporation’s valuation ratios, including its EV/Sales, EV/EBITDA, and Price/Cash Flow ratios, are almost 10 times higher than the sector’s medians.

But the most striking contrast, in my view, is with Tesla’s close peers, namely BYD, Toyota (TM), and General Motors. The former is an EV maker and does not specialize in selling traditional cars. All of Tesla’s valuation ratios are many times higher than those of China’s EV maker BYD. As I have mentioned above, however, China’s EV maker BYD is increasing excellent sales, especially international ones, which is in contrast to Tesla’s poor sales and negative profit growth.

That is why I am saying that Tesla is neither a growth star nor a good buy for a value investor.

Conclusion

But what is my piece of advice on Tesla? Even though I consider the stock to be a terrible investment from a fundamental viewpoint, it is a highly risky idea to sell it short. Why? Despite the falling net income, slowing EV deliveries, and uncertain future of the company’s innovations, the stock is still trading near its all-time highs, suggesting both investors’ and consumers’ love of the company.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Hims & Hers: It's Time To Get Aggressive
data.description:
Over the last few years, I’ve covered Hims & Hers (HIMS) - a fast-growing e-health challenger - a number of times, mostly rating the stock a Strong Buy.

Since my first bullish rating, shares of the company have done quite well, and investors - up until recently - have been rewarded for staying long this high-growth company.

I’m a huge fan of the business, which spends a large amount of money upfront to acquire customers, and then monetizes them over the long term with sticky, high-margin treatments for high-impact health issues like balding, erectile dysfunction, weight loss, and more.

It’s a great model, and the company’s high gross margins give management significant flexibility when it comes to controlling growth and profitability.

Over the last few months, HIMS stock has not been doing well, down more than half from the highs in 2025:

This is largely due to recent profit lumpiness and margin compression, which have tanked investor sentiment. In addition, regulatory uncertainty around the company’s weight loss offering - which has been a strong growth driver in years past - has weighed on shares.

However, I believe that the market has missed the bigger picture here. Recent margin compression has been the result of significant investments in vertical and horizontal scale, which should bear considerable fruit down the road. There's nothing structurally wrong with the business, management is just preparing for the next stage of growth.

Consequently, shares look historically cheap. All told, I believe it’s time to get aggressive in the stock.

Today, I’ll make the case that it’s time to get bullish once again.

Sound good? Let’s dive in.

Financials

Let's begin by taking a look at the business and what HIMS has been up to since my last coverage in April of 2025.

In short, HIMS has been pumping on all cylinders, growing revenues from $401 million in Q3 of 2024 to nearly $600 million in Q3 of 2025:

This strong revenue growth represents a 50% YoY increase, which is incredibly substantial for any business.

My mental model for understanding HIMS is to take the company’s top-line revenue, which has been growing rapidly, and extrapolate it well into the future, given that the business earns primarily recurring revenues from ongoing treatment for chronic conditions like hair loss and ED.

From there, HIMS enjoys gross profit margins in the 70%+ range, showing just how strong the company’s internet-based consultation offering is, and how much of a premium it can charge on primarily generic medicines and treatments:

From there, the company has high SG&A costs, most of which come from the firm’s large marketing budget:

Whatever is left, the company, and shareholders, get to keep as profit.

What encourages me about this business model is that the company’s marketing is working. Over the last year, the company saw strong member growth of roughly 21%, which translated to even stronger revenue growth at the 49% figure I quoted earlier:

Our third quarter results demonstrate continued momentum...

Revenue grew 49% year-over-year, driven by a 21% year-over-year increase in subscribers...

Core to this progress is the growing adoption of personalized solutions, as well as the increasing frequency with which customers rely on our platform to address multiple needs. Subscribers using a personalized treatment plan increased 50% year-over-year, while subscribers utilizing treatment plans to target multiple conditions increased more than 80% year-over-year, now representing over 20% of total subscribers on the platform.

This revenue growth and subscriber growth reflects the fact that monthly online revenue per average subscriber increased 19% year over year, showing that once a customer joins the Hims platform, they begin to order more and more treatments from one centralized, trusted source.

This land-and-expand strategy could drive significant revenue growth going forward.

Combine this economic engine with high gross margins, significant revenue stability, and high marketing spend, and it could drive unbelievable long-term value for shareholders once market share reaches critical mass and management winds down marketing efforts.

Recently, the market has been concerned with HIMS’s results, which showed rapidly increasing operating costs even as gross margins remained flat. This has driven a substantial drop in operating margin over the last two quarters:

This impacts items further down the income statement, including net profit. Given that the company is growing revenues at a 'only' a 50% year-over-year clip, which is slower than in quarter's past, this margin compression has clearly spooked the market, and shares have traded down significantly.

However, the market is missing that this margin compression is not particularly important to the long-term story with HIMS.

Of course, income margins have dropped from 10% to 1% over the last two quarters, but this is largely due to investments HIMS is making into horizontal and vertical scaling.

On the horizontal side, the company is expanding into new markets, including the UK, Europe, and Canada. To do so, it has had to buy and build out local capabilities to get a jumpstart on distribution, marketing, and manufacturing:

In Europe, our acquisition of Zava Global gives us the infrastructure to serve customers across the U.K., Germany, France, Ireland and Spain. We're now reaching a total addressable market of more than 200 million adults across these regions...

Next, we're preparing to launch in Canada in the near future, notably expanding our North America presence. With 2/3 of Canadian adults overweight or living with obesity, we're incredibly excited about this opportunity to make high-touch care more accessible. We are in active discussions with leading generic manufacturers to ensure that once semaglutide becomes available in generic form in 2026, we are ready to deliver access through our platform...

From a vertical perspective, HIMS is also scaling, as it recently invested in GRAIL, a testing company through which it hopes to offer new testing and diagnostic products:

We plan to launch comprehensive lab testing capabilities in the near future, which will pave the way for the launch of our longevity specialty planned for 2026. We believe both will expand our addressable market meaningfully, enabling us to meet customers earlier in their healthcare journey, before conditions arise. Our recent investment in GRAIL reflects this interest in innovations that can shape the future of early detection and screening.

It has also invested in growing its compounded treatment manufacturing facility in Ohio. Management has also outlined plans to grow HIMS into menopause and low testosterone treatments, which I believe could be a new, significant growth market:

Our U.S. operational footprint now spans more than 700,000 square feet, with an additional 350,000 square feet expected to come online when our new Ohio facility is completed.

Given the proven success of the company’s existing business model and telehealth success, I struggle to see how the company will not do well over the very long term as it expands into new product territories once dominated by traditional healthcare providers.

From a growth standpoint, analysts are currently expecting 59% year-over-year growth for full-year 2025, and sales growth of 18% in 2026 and 2027:

However, I believe that these estimates are too low, and that HIMS will likely produce revenue growth in the mid-20% range, which has significant implications for the price of HIMS stock.

Additionally, I believe this 18% figure underrates the company’s growth opportunity, especially with the scaling and cross-selling opportunities management is executing on.

Valuation

Currently, shares of HIMS are trading around 3x sales and 20x free cash flow.

For a company with gross margins above 70% and a recurring revenue model, that appears unbelievably low.

Worries about profitability have sent the stock lower, but as outlined, these should be short-term issues not representative of the company’s long-term earnings potential.

As investments in new global infrastructure, products, and marketing mature, I expect the company’s net margins will expand rapidly toward the 20% mark.

Of course, it’s difficult to say how long it might be until the company’s margins make this move, but if HIMS continues to trade off short-term margins for significant top-line revenue growth, I still believe management is on the right track.

Not only that, but HIMS sells a sticky product that consumers don’t consistently price-shop for, which should drive significant long-term optionality for management as it balances robust growth and margins.

Analyst estimates currently project that HIMS could be at a sub-20x P/E in the next four years, with even better free cash flow economics. Similar to analyst projections around revenue, I expect the street is being overly pessimistic with their expectations:

Assuming the revenue growth trajectory I laid out earlier, I expect that HIMS could be trading at a sub-20x price-to-earnings ratio within the next 24 months. This assumes relatively flat operational costs from here on out through 2027, which allow more margin to flow through to net income.

Bottom line, the company is set up for considerable profitability, but as long as there remain ample opportunities to grow the top line, I’m happy with management making that trade-off. That makes the present valuation, at a low single-digit sales multiple and roughly 20x free cash flow, appear wildly attractive.

As a result, I rate shares of HIMS a Strong Buy.

Risks

I’ve already discussed a number of risks associated with HIMS, but it’s worth outlining a few more so you can get the full picture.

HIMS could face increasing competition as it expands vertically and horizontally. Early results from the UK are promising, but there are already a number of established competitors there, like Manual, who will no doubt compete for the same ad space and offer similar products.

If HIMS cannot replicate the same success it has had in North America elsewhere, it could dent the company’s growth story.

Additionally, HIMS has - in my view - a cheap valuation, but there’s no telling how low the market would be willing to rate the company’s future earnings potential.

As a result, and especially in the event of a market downturn, HIMS could trade lower on a change in sentiment even if the company’s organic business results remain robust.

Summary

Overall, buying HIMS is a relatively risky proposition in a market full of boring, stable companies. However, I believe shares have gotten way too cheap due to short-term issues, and this is a mispricing that should be aggressively taken advantage of.

With a strong business model, great unit economics, and a significant growth trajectory, I see HIMS as an excellent investment opportunity at this price point.

Cheers~

data.title: EICA: Nothing Wrong With Boring
data.description:
Thesis

We recently wrote about the Series C Preferred Shares from Eagle Point Income Company Inc. (EIC) after the Series B was called. Today we are going to look again at the Series A from the same CEF and articulate why these 'boring' securities represent a very constant source of income, with a very low volatility profile.

Why CEF Preferred Shares Exist

CEF preferred shares are a form of funding for the closed-end fund, and they represent what is called 'term funding.' Term funding means that the rate the CEF pays stays the same until the securities are called or mature. CEFs place a mix of true preferred shares (i.e., no maturity date) and term preferred shares (there is a defined maturity date and liability-like treatment on the balance sheet). In the case of EIC, all its series have been term preferred shares.

The details for the Series A (EICA) are as follows:

The term preferred shares have a 5% coupon and are due to mature in October 2026. The series have been callable for a while, but due to their low coupon, the CEF has not done that. A fund will only call term preferred shares when they can place new debt or preferred shares at a much lower rate. It is not the case with these 5% yielding preferred shares.

Why is EICA 'boring'?

We are calling EICA boring because it is trading at $24.8/share with a par value of $25.00 per share. Investors in this security will get $25 per share on October 30, 2026. The upside here is very limited; hence, EICA represents an accrual instrument.

Some yield analytics for the name are as follows:

  • Current yield: 5.04%
  • Yield to maturity: 6.08%
  • Dividend frequency: monthly

Is a 6% yield to maturity something to brag about at a party? No, absolutely not. Does it represent a very valid alternative to money market funds at this stage? You bet.

Keep in mind that EIC is a closed-end fund, thus subject to the 1940 Act regulations, which offer coverage requirements for preferred shares. In other words, the credit risk associated with EICA is very, very small. Hence, the securities are more of an alternative to short-dated funds and money market funds.

What is the CEF Going to Do Next?

This is the most interesting question for us since the fund must issue liabilities or preferred shares in order to keep its current gearing ratio. EICB was replaced with privately placed securities, which were issued at a better rate.

If we look at what other options the CEF has, we will see that the bank facility is fairly small as per the Semi-Annual Report:

Pursuant to the terms of the Revolving Credit Facility, the Company can borrow up to an aggregate principal balance of $25.0 million (the “Commitment Amount”). The Revolving Credit facility is collateralized by certain investments held by the Company. The Company has granted a security interest in certain assets to BNP Paribas, as lender. Such borrowings under the Revolving Credit Facility bear interest at Term SOFR plus a spread. The Company is required to pay a commitment fee on the unused amount. For the six months ended June 30, 2025, the Company had an average outstanding borrowing and average interest rate of approximately $1.38 million and 6.49%, respectively.

So given the above, we see a rough implied spread of 2% here for the revolving facility. The coupon, though, is still above the 5% that EICA is paying. We are of the opinion that the CEF will try to place new term securities somewhere in the 6% to 6.5% range, depending on tenor and other details.

It might be, though, that new securities will not be exchange listed, but rather privately placed. While funding oneself via SOFR-based funding (i.e., the bank facility) is advantageous in a decreasing rates environment like now, we have to point out that the balance for the facility is small, and securities are pledged here, some of which might require mark-to-market. This simply means the fund has to post more collateral if the market crashes, which would be a liquidity constraint. Therefore, placing a new term paper is the most elegant solution here.

What Are the Alternatives?

We are currently thinking about EICA in the context of money market funds and short-term funds. We have covered a number of them, so please find below a cohort of peers, but do note they all offer yields that are sub-5%:

  • TBUX: Continues To Be A Buy In A Liquidity Constrained World
  • TFLO: When In Doubt, Cash Is The Answer
  • GSY: Still A Robust Invesco Fund, Still A Buy

While short-term treasury funds run no risks, the bond ones like TBUX do have very small credit profiles; however, they are very much comparable with EICA but yield less.

Conclusion

EICA is a term for preferred security from a fixed-income CEF. The maturity date is October 30, 2026, thus creating a very short-dated risk profile. Due to the 1940 Act, the CEF has to maintain coverage ratios for the security; thus, the credit risk profile is very benign. With a 6.08% yield to maturity, EICA competes with short-term bond funds and money market funds but offers roughly 100 bps more in spread. We still very much like this 'boring' security in 2026.
